</h2>

<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p>In Hollywood the scientific term for that part of the story where "things get interesting" is: "the call to adventure". It's the moment where you, the hero, has to decide whether to keep having your boring ass life or if you're gonna step outside your comfort zone.</p><p>Every day you get 3-4 "calls-to-adventure". But if you never act on them. You'll stop noticing them.</p><p>The first thing you gotta know about Drive-By photoshoots is... You can't overthink it. As soon as you get the inspiration to approach someone you have to act on it immediately. Otherwise, you'll talk yourself out of it. </p><p>If you're lucky, every once in a while, the person you approach will suggest an idea that's out of your comfort zone. Two seconds ago they were a complete stranger. Now they are a co-conspirator who thinks you should "break into the church across the street and shoot on the roof". Yes, it happens that fast. If you connect, you connect. And if you haven't experienced this yet, it's only because you haven't started doing drive-bys yet. (More on that later.)</p><p>That part inside of you that's questioning "can I trust this person that wants me to go on an adventure with them"-- That part is called 'The Liar'. Don't listen to 'The Liar'. People (yes, we all do it) use lack of trust as an excuse for not following up on things. Babe, it's not a trust issue. It's a fear issue. You're just scared. </p><p>Remember what the opposite of fear is?</p><p>Love.</p><p>Stop judging things that haven't even happened yet.</p><p>It doesn't matter if it turns out great or if it becomes an awkward-fest. What matters is that you have a story to go home with. Never shy away from situations that you can learn from. Embrace every moment that you can add to the highlight reel that will flash before your eyes one day when you're on your death bed.</p><p>But for now... I just want to remind you that it's ok to get outside of your comfort zone. It's okay to be uncomfortable. You don't have to course correct everything.</p><p>I say this because as you're developing your "guts", you'll find that you always come to point where you have one last chance to change your mind. To jump ship. And 90% of people do. That's why 90% of people don't live up to their full potential. I'm not saying that you need put yourself in danger. But I am saying take calculated risks. Especially if you're a creative soul. Calculated risks are the seeds of your art.</p><p>People try so hard to normalize the weirdness they feel when they meet someone for the first time. They're in a rush to get back to safety. And because of that, they never give themselves the time to appreciate the other person as a blank canvas. They ruin the moment with a million questions and small talk in desperation to find common ground. When the best common ground, when you're truly comfortable with yourself and the world around you... Is silence.</p><p>The moment you stop overthinking and you just decide (and yes, it IS a choice) to just let go... To enjoy the ride... To let others point the direction forward... That's when you'll start to see all the previously invisible connections that exist between things.</p><p>But some people will never let go. They call it "pride"... Like it's a good thing.</p><p>I call it what it is:</p><p>Fear.</p><p>Remember what the opposite of fear is?</p><p>Love.</p><h1><br></h1><h1><strong>ACTION:</strong></h1><p>Go out right now and look around for places that would be cool to shoot. Do this for 30 minutes.</p><p>After those precious 30 minutes of "location casting", ask 3 complete strangers if you can take a picture of them.</p><p>Back to back.</p><p>If you connect with one of them (and you never know... It might even be the first one!)... Turn your little "photo-moment" that you guys are experiencing into a "photoshoot" right on the spot. </p><p>Take your new muse to the locations you found previously for even more interesting shots.</p><p>And now for the clincher that I hope gets you into some good "trouble".</p><p>Let them suggest somewhere you should go that they think is interesting.</p><p>That's when the adventure begins.</p><p></p>
</div>
